title: Object-Format Transition, Compatibility, and Disaster Defense · Graph Retrieval
summary: A delayed handoff reconstructs object-format transition, compatibility, and disaster defense from terse repository evidence, challenges one retained misconception, and revises the Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain SHA-1 and SHA-256 repository object formats, repository initialization choice, ID length, storage identity, and why algorithms are not a cosmetic display option.
Evaluate compatibility object formats, translation tables, protocol exchange, clone and fetch interoperability, tooling assumptions, and repository immutability limits.
Inventory scripts, parsers, databases, APIs, hooks, CI, hosting, and documentation for Git 3.0 breaking defaults, long IDs, and version-gated behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Retrieve the complete evidence cycle
steps.instruction: Order the Gita Object Format Sha256 evidence cycle for this review task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Gita Object Format Sha256 assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: Case G94 is a review in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Gita Object Format Sha256 has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Gita Object Format Sha256 precondition or invariant that the review case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Case G94 is a review in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Changing objectFormat rewrites only configuration while preserving every existing object ID.

options.text: Inspect repository boundary, config origin, current operation, worktree, index, refs, and graph
Preserve uncommitted work and a recovery ref, then state intended tree and topology
Preview and perform the least destructive simulated Git operation
Verify status, staged and range diffs, graph, tests, remote target, and recovery path

content.title: Evidence defense
